title: React Context API
summary: Context API, Provider patterns, context composition, and avoiding the performance pitfalls of over-using context.

questions.prompt: What problem does React Context primarily solve?
choices: Slow renders in deeply nested trees
Prop drilling — threading data through intermediate components that don't need it
Sharing functions between sibling components
Global async state management
questions.explanation: Context lets you broadcast a value to any component in the tree without manually passing it through each level. The classic problem is 'prop drilling' — intermediate components that serve only as pass-throughs for data they don't use.

questions.prompt: When a Context Provider's value changes, which components re-render?
choices: Only the Provider component
All components that called useContext() for that context — regardless of which part of the value they use
Only direct children of the Provider
Components are smart enough to skip if their slice didn't change
questions.explanation: React re-renders ALL consumers of the context when the value changes — it doesn't selectively update only consumers that read the changed part. This is why passing large objects in a single context can cause performance issues.

questions.prompt: What performance problem does this pattern have?
choices: No problem — this is the correct pattern
Every render creates a new `{ user, logout }` object — all consumers re-render even when user and logout haven't changed
setUser is not available to consumers
Provider without a key prop causes issues
questions.explanation: `{ user, logout }` is a new object literal on every render. Even if `user` is the same value and `logout` is stable (from useCallback), the wrapper object is a new reference. Context comparison uses Object.is on the value — new reference = all consumers re-render.

questions.prompt: The 'separate state from dispatch' pattern for context means:
choices: Never use dispatch with context
Put state in one context and dispatch/actions in another — components that only dispatch won't re-render when state changes
Use useState for state and useReducer for dispatch in the same context
Export action creators separately
questions.explanation: Dispatch from useReducer is always stable (same reference). If you put dispatch in its own context, components that only dispatch (like buttons) never re-render when state changes — they only subscribed to the dispatch context which never changes.

questions.prompt: Why wrap useContext in a custom hook like `useAuth()`?
choices: Required by TypeScript
To validate context is used inside its Provider (throw clear error) and hide implementation details from consumers
To make context reactive to changes
To prevent re-renders
questions.explanation: Without the custom hook, consumers get `null` if used outside the Provider (when defaulting to null). The custom hook adds `if (!ctx) throw new Error(...)` — a clear, actionable error message. It also gives a stable API — the context implementation can change without touching every consumer.

questions.prompt: Context's default value (second arg to createContext) is used:
choices: When the Provider's value prop is undefined
When a component calls useContext but there is no matching Provider anywhere above it in the tree
As the initial state before first render
When the context value is null
questions.explanation: The default value is a fallback for components rendered outside any Provider. If `createContext(null)` and a component calls `useContext(AuthContext)` with no Provider above it, it gets `null` — the default. Commonly used in testing or libraries to provide safe defaults.

questions.prompt: Should you use context for rapidly changing data like mouse position?
choices: Yes — context is perfect for frequently updating data
No — every change re-renders all consumers; mouse position can change 60+ times per second causing severe performance issues
Yes if you memoize the value
Yes if you use useLayoutEffect to read it
questions.explanation: Context triggers re-renders in all consumers on every change. Mouse position updating at 60Hz means 60 full re-renders per second for every consumer. For high-frequency data, use a ref (no re-render), a subscription pattern, or a purpose-built library like Zustand.

questions.prompt: Which are good use cases for React Context?
choices: Authentication state (current user, logout function)
UI theme (light/dark mode)
Every keystroke in a search input
Locale/language preference
questions.explanation: Auth, theme, and locale change infrequently and are needed across the app — ideal for context. Keystroke-level data changes many times per second and is local to one component — it belongs in useState close to where it's used, not a global context.

questions.prompt: To fix the 'new object on every render' context bug, the best approach is:
choices: Wrap the Provider in React.memo
Use useMemo to memoize the context value, or split into separate state/dispatch contexts
Pass a string instead of an object
List the context in a dep array
questions.explanation: `const value = useMemo(() => ({ user, logout }), [user, logout])` — only creates a new object when `user` or `logout` actually changes. Alternatively, split into `<UserContext.Provider value={user}><ActionsContext.Provider value={{ logout }}>` so they update independently.

questions.prompt: Does React.memo on a consumer component prevent context-triggered re-renders?
choices: Yes — React.memo skips all re-renders
No — React.memo only compares props. Context changes bypass React.memo
Only if the context value is a primitive
Yes if the context value is memoized
questions.explanation: React.memo wraps a component and skips re-rendering if props are unchanged. But when a context value changes, React directly re-renders ALL consumers regardless of React.memo. memo protects against PARENT re-renders propagating down — not against context updates.

questions.prompt: Multiple context values can be composed cleanly by:
choices: Passing all values in one mega-context
Creating a Providers wrapper component that nests all Provider components — used once at the app root
Using provider chaining via the extends keyword
Using a flat array of contexts
questions.explanation: A dedicated `<Providers>` wrapper component nests all providers internally. The app root uses `<Providers><App /></Providers>` — one clean line. Adding a new provider means editing one file. This is a common pattern in large React apps.

questions.prompt: What does this component render when used outside any ThemeContext.Provider?
choices: An error — no Provider found
Button with className='light' — uses the default value
Button with no className
null
questions.explanation: When no Provider is found, useContext returns the default value passed to createContext — here `'light'`. The button renders with `className='light'`. No error. This can be a silent bug if the default is null and you're not doing a null check.

questions.prompt: Context is NOT a replacement for which of these?
choices: Prop drilling
Global theme state
Server-state management (caching fetched API data, background refetching, optimistic updates)
Locale settings
questions.explanation: Context is for synchronous state that lives in the React tree. Server state — fetched data, caching, background updates, invalidation — has a very different lifecycle. Libraries like React Query / TanStack Query or SWR are designed for server state. Using context for it leads to complex stale-data bugs.

questions.prompt: If Component A provides a UserContext, and Component B renders inside A but provides its OWN UserContext.Provider, components inside B read:
choices: A's context — providers don't override
B's context — the nearest Provider wins
Both values merged
An error — duplicate Providers are not allowed
questions.explanation: Context reads from the nearest Provider above in the tree. This lets you intentionally override context for a subtree — common in testing (wrapping a component in a mock provider) or in UI libraries that may need their own isolated context.

questions.prompt: Which approaches prevent unnecessary re-renders when a context value changes?
choices: Memoize the context value with useMemo
Split into separate contexts for different data shapes
Use React.memo on the Provider
Keep context values as primitives when possible (primitives compare by value)
questions.explanation: Memoized values (A), split contexts (B), and primitive values (D) all reduce unnecessary re-renders. React.memo on the Provider (C) prevents the Provider's own re-render if its props haven't changed — but the consumers still re-render when the context value changes.

questions.prompt: The cleanest TypeScript pattern for a required context (no safe default) is:
choices: createContext<AuthState>(undefined as any)
createContext<AuthState | null>(null) — then in a custom hook, throw if null
createContext<AuthState>({} as AuthState)
Avoid TypeScript for contexts
questions.explanation: `createContext<AuthState | null>(null)` is correct TypeScript — the default is null (no Provider). The custom hook `useAuth` reads the context and throws `new Error('must be inside AuthProvider')` if it's null. This gives both correct types and a helpful runtime error.

questions.prompt: Component composition (passing JSX as children/slots) vs Context: when is comp composition better?
choices: Component composition is always better
When the data only needs to travel 1-2 levels deep, passing JSX itself avoids both prop drilling and context overhead
Context is always better than composition
Composition requires Redux
questions.explanation: If you have `<Page><Avatar user={user} /></Page>` where page doesn't use user, consider: `<Page element={<Avatar user={user} />} />` — pass the composed JSX. This way user stays in the component that creates Avatar and Page never sees it. No prop drilling, no context.

questions.prompt: How many times does ChildB re-render when count changes in this setup?
choices: 1 — ChildB re-renders because count changed
0 — ChildB only reads SetterCtx which never changes
2 — once for each context
It depends on React version
questions.explanation: ChildB only subscribes to SetterCtx. setCount (from useState) has a stable reference — it never changes. When count updates, ValueCtx changes (ChildA re-renders) but SetterCtx does NOT change — ChildB skips the re-render. This is the exact benefit of splitting state and setter contexts.

questions.prompt: Can context replace all uses of component props?
choices: Yes — context is more powerful
No — props make data flow explicit and are better for data specific to a component pair. Context is for cross-cutting concerns
Yes if using TypeScript
Only for primitive values
questions.explanation: Props are intentionally explicit — you can see exactly what data a component receives. This is valuable for maintainability. Context hides data flow, making it harder to trace where a value comes from. Use props as the default; reach for context only when prop drilling becomes painful (>3 levels, many components).

questions.prompt: What happens if you call `useContext(SomeContext)` outside a React function component?
choices: Returns the default value
Throws a hooks rule violation — hooks can only be called inside React function components or custom hooks
Returns undefined
Reads from the global context store
questions.explanation: useContext is a hook — subject to all rules of hooks. It can only be called inside React function components or inside other hooks. Calling it in a regular function, class component, or outside React throws an invalid hook call error.

flashcards.back: ALL components that useContext() for a context re-render when its value changes — regardless of which slice they read. Split contexts to limit blast radius.
`const val = useMemo(() => ({ user, logout }), [user, logout])` — prevents new object on every render. Alternative: split into separate contexts.
No matching Provider above in the tree. Not when value is undefined/null. Always provide a safe default or throw in a custom hook wrapper.
dispatch from useReducer is always stable. Put dispatch in its own context. Components that only dispatch never re-render when state changes.
React.memo blocks parent-triggered re-renders but NOT context-triggered ones. Context changes always reach consumers directly.
Wrap `useContext` in `useFoo()`. Check for null and throw. Hides implementation, gives clear error when used outside Provider, stable consumer API.
Context for synchronous app state (auth, theme). NOT for server state (fetched data). Use React Query or SWR for caching, background refetching, stale data.
useContext reads from the closest Provider above in the tree. Works intentionally for test overrides and library isolation.

project.brief: Design a multi-context app shell for a SaaS dashboard with three cross-cutting concerns: 1) AuthContext — current user, login, logout. 2) ThemeContext — light/dark/system preference with persistence to localStorage. 3) NotificationContext — list of toast messages, actions: addNotification, dismissNotification. For each: describe the context shape, how the Provider stores state (useState or useReducer?), the custom hook API consumers use, and what value is passed to the Provider. Then address: how would you prevent NotificationContext updates from re-rendering the AuthContext consumers?

checklist.text: AuthContext uses { user, login, logout } — memoised value or split state/actions contexts
ThemeContext uses useState + localStorage sync in useEffect, exposes { theme, setTheme }
NotificationContext uses useReducer for list management — ADD and DISMISS actions
Each context has a custom hook (useAuth, useTheme, useNotifications) that throws outside its Provider
Explains isolation: separate contexts = separate subscriptions = notifications don't trigger auth consumers

hints: Auth: split into UserContext (value={user}) and AuthActionsContext (value={login, logout}) — login/logout can be stable useCallback.
Theme: const [theme, setTheme] = useState(() => localStorage.getItem('theme') ?? 'light') — lazy init from storage.
Notifications: useReducer with { notifications: Notification[] } state — dispatch ADD/DISMISS actions.
Isolation: three completely separate Provider trees. NotificationContext changes only re-render notification consumers.
